paper_title: Invisible Injections: Unicode-Based Adversarial Attacks on Tool-Integrated LLM Agents

ground_truth.abstract:
text[[86, 209, 488, 485]]
Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into agent frameworks with tool invocation capabilities, creating new attack surfaces for malicious actors. This paper proposes a novel category of prompt injection attacks- Ghost Prompt Injection, targeting tool- integrated LLM agents by exploiting specific Unicode character properties, including invisible tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional (BIDI) control characters. These attacks leverage the discrepancy between human visual perception and machine tokenization: while the adversarial prompts remain visually indistinguishable from benign instructions to human observers, they contain covert payloads that hijack the agent's execution logic. We define two primary attack vectors- insertion- based attacks using invisible characters and substitution- based attacks using homoglyphs- to achieve malicious objectives such as information leakage, denial of service, and content manipulation. We evaluated these vulnerabilities across three prominent agent platforms (Claude Desktop, Augment, and Cline). Our experiments demonstrate that leading proprietary models fail to detect these Unicode- based injections, resulting in the successful execution of unauthorized tasks, such as sending tampered emails and navigating to phishing websites.

sub_title[[86, 497, 200, 511]]
ground_truth.introduction_last_third:
combining rule-based sanitization with a Fine-tuning-based Detection Model. This hybrid approach effectively mitigates risks by simultaneously filtering anomalous characters and verifying semantic intent.

text[[544, 668, 928, 791]]
- Extensive empirical evaluation and real-world risk quantification: We systematically assessed GPI across major agent frameworks, revealing severe DoS risks (latency surging to 262s) and pervasive logic vulnerabilities (e.g., 60-80% for BIDI controls). Furthermore, by analyzing a constructed corpus of 54,355 real-world instructions, we identified and verified 74 active malicious samples, confirming the immediate presence of these threats in open-source ecosystems.

sub_title[[517, 814, 644, 830]]
ground_truth.conclusion:
text[[518, 179, 913, 329]]
In this paper, we uncover the "Perception- Semantic Gap" in tool- integrated LLM agents, demonstrating how Ghost Prompt Injection (GPI) exploits visual- logical discrepancies to execute invisible Denial of Service (DoS) and logic hijacking attacks. Furthermore, to detect these widespread vulnerabilities, we propose GPIGuard, a hybrid framework that combines codepoint auditing with semantic verification. Validated on real- world data, GPIGuard effectively filters benign encoding noise while neutralizing malicious payloads, which establishes a critical defense baseline for securing the logical integrity of autonomous agent instructions against imperceptible threats.

sub_title[[86, 105, 297, 120]]

original_summary: The paper introduces Ghost Prompt Injection (GPI), a novel attack paradigm that exploits Unicode anomalies—such as invisible tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters—to create visually benign yet malicious prompts that hijack tool-integrated LLM agents by exploiting the perception-semantic gap between human vision and machine tokenization. To counter this threat, the authors propose GPIGuard, a hybrid detection framework that combines deterministic codepoint-level sanitization with a fine-tuned LLM-based semantic verifier, effectively distinguishing malicious payloads from legitimate multilingual inputs while minimizing false positives. Through extensive evaluation on real-world instructions and major agent platforms, they demonstrate widespread vulnerabilities, including latency spikes up to 262 seconds and 60–80% success rates for BIDI-based attacks, and identify 74 active malicious samples in open ecosystems, establishing GPIGuard as a critical, empirically validated defense baseline against imperceptible prompt injection threats.

original_validation.critique: The summary accurately reflects the core contributions and findings of the paper, including the introduction of Ghost Prompt Injection, the proposal of GPIGuard, and the empirical evaluation results. No significant omissions or unsupported claims were detected.

final_summary: The paper introduces Ghost Prompt Injection (GPI), a novel attack paradigm that exploits Unicode anomalies—such as invisible tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters—to create visually benign yet malicious prompts that hijack tool-integrated LLM agents by exploiting the perception-semantic gap between human vision and machine tokenization. To counter this threat, the authors propose GPIGuard, a hybrid detection framework that combines deterministic codepoint-level sanitization with a fine-tuned LLM-based semantic verifier, effectively distinguishing malicious payloads from legitimate multilingual inputs while minimizing false positives. Through extensive evaluation on real-world instructions and major agent platforms, they demonstrate widespread vulnerabilities, including latency spikes up to 262 seconds and 60–80% success rates for BIDI-based attacks, and identify 74 active malicious samples in open ecosystems, establishing GPIGuard as a critical, empirically validated defense baseline against imperceptible prompt injection threats.

final_validation.critique: The summary accurately reflects the core contributions and findings of the paper, including the introduction of Ghost Prompt Injection, the proposal of GPIGuard, and the empirical evaluation results. No significant omissions or unsupported claims were detected.
